context: In Obadiah, notice, we won't dig real deep into this, but notice what the Bible says in verse number one.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: The vision of Obadiah, thus saith the Lord God concerning Edom...
point_summary: The entire book of Obadiah is shown to be the long-range fulfillment of God's prenatal oracle to Rebekah, as its singular subject is the judgment pronounced against Edom, the nation descended from Esau.

context: For thy violence against thy brother Jacob, shame shall cover thee...
point_summary: The entire minor prophet Obadiah is shown to be the long-range fulfillment of God's prenatal word to Rebekah, as Edom's violence against brother Jacob brings the divine verdict of being cut off forever.

context: And just one chapter in the book of Obadiah, and we'll look at just a few verses, really as a springboard into our message this evening.
point_summary: Obadiah's single-chapter prophecy is addressed to Edom—descendants of Esau—a nation historically hostile toward Israel, setting the stage for God's judgment on their pride.

context: "...the vision of Obadiah, thus saith the Lord God concerning Edom, we have heard a rumor from the Lord, an ambassador is sent among the heathen, arise ye and let us rise up against her in battle. Behold, I have made thee small among the heathen. Thou art greatly despised."
point_summary: Obadiah 1:1 frames the entire book as a divine oracle of certain doom and judgment against Edom, the descendants of Esau, who were historic adversaries of Israel.

context: The pride of thine heart hath deceived thee. Notice what it says, Thou that dwellest in the clefts of the rock, whose habitation is high, but then notice this last part of verse number 3, that saith in his heart, who shall bring me down to the ground?
point_summary: Pride's deceptive power is seen in Edom's boast — "who shall bring me down?" — which the sermon connects to inordinate self-esteem, the very sin found in Lucifer's heart.

Self-deception about one's own spiritual strength—thinking oneself to be something when one is nothing—is the heart of the pride condemned in Obadiah 3, linked here to Galatians 6:3 and 1 Corinthians 10:12.

context: it's very interesting if you couple this verse with where we started in the book of Obadiah and I mentioned as we look there at the at the book of Obadiah in that third verse
point_summary: Pride's self-sufficiency—"who shall bring me down?"—is shown to mirror the Laodicean church's boast of needing nothing, linking Edom's deceived heart to the danger of spiritual self-reliance.
